edus.text: WEST DES MOINES , Iowa-President Trump and former Vice President Joe Biden exchanged barbs from opposite ends of Iowa on Tuesday ,
offering a preview of a potential general election face-off .
The two candidates appeared for the first time in the same state
as the 2020 race takes shape ,
both looking beyond the Democratic presidential primary to the general election .
Mr. Trump aims to energize his core supporters in a state
he easily carried in 2016
but where his aggressive tariff moves have disrupted business for farmers
and drawn bipartisan criticism from elected officials .
Mr. Biden is making the case
that he is the Democrat
best equipped to defeat Mr. Trump .
While both sought to talk policy ,
neither could resist taking some shots at each other .
Mr. Biden offered an extensive takedown of the president ' s time in the White House before a cheering crowd in Iowa ,
using Mr. Trump ' s own words as evidence of why Democrats should " clearly and firmly reject " the president .
Share Your Thoughts
How do you think
President Trump ' s farm policies will affect his re-election effort in Iowa ?
Join the conversation below .
" ' I have absolute power. '
No , you don ' t , Donald Trump !
' Only I can fix it.
' Fix yourself first , Donald Trump , "
Mr. Biden said before a cheering crowd in Davenport .
Earlier in the day , the former vice president made a passing reference to Mr. Trump ' s 2016 campaign slogan ,
saying in Mount Pleasant :
" You talk about the fact
that let ' s ' Make America Great Again. '
I ' d settle for just let America be America again . "
From a renewable-energy plant in Council Bluffs on the western edge of the state , Mr. Trump largely focused on his administration ' s ethanol policies and other support for farmers
but made a few comments about Mr. Biden ,
whom he has dubbed " Sleepy Joe . "
Mr. Trump said of Mr. Biden ,
" People don ' t respect him. "
He added
that Mr. Biden ' s repeated references to him " reminds me of Crooked Hillary , " in a swipe at his 2016 Democratic opponent , Hillary Clinton .
At a state Republican dinner of more than 700 Republicans in West Des Moines , Mr. Trump ticked off what he sees as his administration ' s key achievements ,
including his efforts on trade and immigration , as well as two Supreme Court judges .
He also delivered a series of attack lines against Democrats on their abortion and environmental policies
and labeled the party " socialists "
but avoided mentioning Mr. Biden .
The clash between the two began before the day ' s events ,
as Mr. Biden ' s team released prepared remarks
that included a criticism of the president ' s policies and style ,
calling him an " existential threat to America . "
Mr. Trump shot back ,
telling reporters at the White House
that Mr. Biden was the " weakest , mentally "
and that
" when he mentions my name that many times ,
I guess
they should be complimented . "
While Mr. Biden zeroed in on the president in his second trip to early-voting Iowa
since he declared his candidacy ,
he is locked in a competitive Democratic primary battle against 22 rivals .
Polls show him as the front-runner ,
but his more-moderate policy approach has been criticized by other Democratic hopefuls , as has his limited campaign schedule .
Most of the field courted party activists at a weekend event in Iowa
that
Mr. Biden said
he couldn ' t attend
because of his granddaughter ' s high-school graduation .
A poll of 600 likely Democratic Iowa caucus-goers
released over the weekend
showed Mr. Biden leading the field with 24 % .
The survey ,
sponsored by the Des Moines Register , CNN and Mediacom ,
showed
Vermont Sen. Bernie Sanders supported by 16 % ,
followed by Massachusetts Sen. Elizabeth Warren at 15 % , South Bend , Ind. , Mayor Pete Buttigieg at 14 % , and Sen. Kamala Harris of California at 7 % .
No one else topped 2 % .
The poll had a margin of error of plus or minus 4 percentage points .
Mr. Trump started his Iowa visit at an official event in Council Bluffs on his administration ' s ethanol policies ,
followed by a state Republican Party dinner in West Des Moines .
Mr. Biden was campaigning in Ottumwa , Mount Pleasant and Davenport on Tuesday and Clinton on Wednesday .
In keeping with Mr. Biden ' s focus on Mr. Trump ,
three of the four campaign events
he scheduled on Tuesday and Wednesday
were to be held in counties
the president easily won in 2016 .
The first was in Wapello County ,
which gave Mr. Trump 57.5 % of the vote ,
while his second was in Henry County ,
where the president won 61.5 % .
Mr. Biden ' s two other campaign events were set for eastern Iowa counties
that will also likely be critical to Democratic efforts
to win the state in the 2020 general election .
Scott County narrowly backed Hillary Clinton in 2016 , 46.9 % to 45.4 % ,
while Mr. Trump won Clinton County by more than 5 percentage points .
In Ottumwa , Mr. Biden ratcheted up his critique of Mr. Trump ,
assailing the president ' s trade dispute with China , his commitment
to fighting climate change
and protecting health-care benefits .
Mr. Biden said
Iowa farmers had been " crushed by his tariff war with China "
and accused the president of backing off his threat of tariffs on Mexico
" because he realized
that he was likely to lose Michigan , Ohio and Iowa . "
" To him , American workers , in my view , are just a pawn-just a pawn in his game , "
Mr. Biden said .
Antiabortion activists heckled Mr. Biden at all of his Iowa events Tuesday
after he reversed his position last week
to now oppose a ban on the use of federal funds for most abortions .
One protester repeatedly trailed him at the events ,
yelling ,
" What about the babies , Joe ? "
Pushing back in Council Bluffs against Mr. Biden ' s criticisms ,
Mr. Trump stressed his administration ' s recent approval of the expanded use of corn-based ethanol in gasoline
and defended his trade moves ,
saying
he was " reversing decades of failed trade policies . "
" Every day we ' re working
to deliver for America ' s farmers ,
just as they work every day
to deliver for us , "
Mr. Trump , said
before signing an executive order
aimed at helping agricultural biotechnology production .
The president ' s trip to Iowa underscores
that he plans to leave nothing to chance in a state
he captured by nearly 10 percentage points in 2016 over Mrs. Clinton
but that Barack Obama won in 2008 and 2012 .
Heading into 2020 ,
polling suggests
that the president ' s support among Iowa Republicans has held firm .
But it is unclear whether the electorate as a whole supports his performance .
" There ' s some angst among farmers ,
but these farmers , their support seems to be sticking with him , "
he said .
